chapters.title: Stepping out of the routine
chapters.text:
Living with a SEAL reveals the benefit of going out of one’s comfort zone to achieve great results. Anyone can find themselves in a tricky situation when the greatest urge is to give up. But hard work and determination always pay off. The stronger your will is, the more remarkable results you achieve. The story of Jesse Itzler teaches us this lesson. His experience is an excellent example of sticking to discipline to reach your goals.

Jesse Itzler loves routine so much that he ran the New York Marathon 18 times in a row, with the same drills yearly. His training schedule and running route remained unchanged; he bought bananas and ordered pizza from the same place before every race. Although routine can be good, especially regarding physical fitness, doing the same every time can lead to a rut. Itzler continued repeating his exercises until he began to feel like he was living his life on autopilot, and there was no improvement. Finally, he left his pattern to live with a Navy SEAL for 30 days. He invited the SEAL to train him at his home in December 2010, but the training was not for a trophy or protection. The goal was to improve mentally, physically, and spiritually.

Breaking a routine is sometimes good because it keeps you open to new ideas.

Jesse Itzler teaches us that most successes in life tie in to learning how to be comfortable in any situation. Furthermore, he shows that going outside the box is sometimes necessary compared to sticking to a routine.

If you can see yourself doing something, you can do it. If you can't see yourself doing something, usually you can't achieve it. ~ SEAL

chapters.title: Embracing the challenge
chapters.text:
Jesse Itzler was on an ultra-marathon team of six people running a footrace longer than the traditional marathon of 42.195 kilometers. It was in that race in San Diego that he met SEAL. Many teams from all over the country registered for the race, with friends coming together to test their abilities. However, SEAL didn't have a team and no friends to support him; he ran the entire race alone.

SEAL wasn’t like the rest. He was the only Black man, and he was also much bigger compared to the others. There was a difference in behavior; everyone was open and cheerful, yet SEAL looked furious, waiting for the race to start. When it did, Itzler couldn't help but notice the sense of purpose with which SEAL ran; it was as though his life depended on it. When the race was over, Itzler met with SEAL to ask about training with him. SEAL agreed on the condition that Itzler would do everything he said, and he agreed.

Control your mind by vocalizing what you want to see in every situation.

The day SEAL was to arrive, Jesse Itzler was very anxious and checked the guest room to ensure it was in perfect shape. He was overwhelmed because SEAL didn't give him any information about his trip; he just said he would be at his house around 7:00 a.m. So, everything between 6:38 a.m. to 7:00 a.m. felt like forever. Finally, SEAL arrived precisely at 7:00 a.m. with no luggage, suitcase, or coat despite the freezing weather. Immediately after SEAL's arrival, they headed to Central Park, ran six miles at a 9:20-mile pace, and ended with Itzler doing 100 pull-ups.

He welcomed day two by running 12 miles in freezing rain; on the third day, they ran 14.3 miles. To get Itzler out of his comfort zone, SEAL made him sleep on a chair on day three.

Despite all odds, he was determined to finish SEAL’s challenges.

chapters.title: Honing the body and the mind
chapters.text:
On day three, Jesse Itzler woke up in the chair at around 5:30 a.m. in extreme pain. He agreed to meet with SEAL at 6:00 a.m. for his fitness test. They went to the gym at Itzler's house and walked to the pull-up bar for what SEAL calls “nickels and dimes.” Nickels were five pull-ups, while dimes meant ten push-ups. The workout started each time the second hand was on the 12; if the session finished in 40 seconds, there would be a 20-second break. When 10 minutes had passed, they would have done 50 pull-ups and 100 push-ups. However, Itzler's pull-up count dropped to three at four minutes because he couldn't keep up.

At the end of day four, Jesse Itzler had run for six miles and completed 36 pull-ups, a 15-minute treadmill test, 50 box jumps, and 100 push-ups. All through the training, SEAL kept a journal of everything Itzler did.

SEAL believes that when people think they are done, they are at 40% of their body capabilities because they have limited themselves.

On day five, they did 500 workouts (bench presses, lat pull-downs, and shoulder presses) and 150 push-ups. On his last morning run on day six, Itzler ran the first three miles at a ten-minute pace, the next two at an eight-minute rate, and the final mile at a seven-minute pace. After his morning runs, he had a routine of bathing, eating a bowl of fruit with bananas, and then going to work. When he got to work on day six, SEAL came over and asked him to do the burpee test — 100 burpees in 10-13 minutes. He was reluctant because he was in his work clothes, but before he could object, the clock was already ticking. He completed the test in 11 minutes and 45 seconds, trembling with shaky legs. Later that night, on day six, SEAL and Itzler went to Central Park for a run and did 12 miles (six miles “escalation” pace), 300 push-ups, and 100 burpees. SEAL felt Itzler's runs were predictable, so he made him run for 17 miles and do 275 push-ups the following morning. SEAL was an exemplary trainer who taught Itzler to do more than he ever imagined he could.

chapters.title: The key to excelling at anything
chapters.text:
When the training reached the 13th day, Jesse Itzler woke up with pains in his shoulders from the 484 push-ups he had done the previous day. Regardless of the pain, he went out with SEAL for a five-mile run at an eight-minute pace. After the run, Itzler decided to work from home because of the pain. There was no workout for the entire afternoon and evening, which was a big surprise. Unfortunately, he didn't know that SEAL had set his alarm for 12:30 a.m. So, he woke Itzler from sleep and hijacked him for a three-mile run, 800 jumping jacks, and 200 push-ups in 20-degree weather.

The next day, at 09:45 a.m., SEAL announced they would do a basic military workout called fireman carries. Itzler was to take SEAL over his shoulder while running 30 yards in the basement hallway, drop him and do 35 reps of flutter kicks, flip to his knees, and do 20 push-ups; then they switched, and SEAL carried him. They repeated the fireman 14 times. At 3:00 p.m., Itzler and SEAL were at the office, and SEAL asked him to do burpees. He was able to do 100 burpees in 10 minutes and 27 seconds.

After all the stress Itzler went through at work, SEAL demanded an eight-mile run. Itzler pleaded with him to make it four miles, but he refused. They later agreed to run for six miles at around 11:45 p.m. At the end of everything, Itzler was in bad shape with extreme shoulder pain.

SEAL believes you get more out of 10 push-ups the right way than 30 done wrongly.

On the 15th day, Jesse Itzler ran for eight miles and felt great about his improvement. Then, he, his wife Sara, and SEAL traveled to Atlanta to check on Sara's company, Spanx. On getting to Atlanta, SEAL ensured Itzler did 350 push-ups, and they went out for a three-mile run the following day. When they returned to New York, SEAL got a metal-plated weight vest package, and Itzler ran for 17 miles, doing 5.5 miles in the 50-pound vest.

chapters.title: Daily exercise will help you manage your weight
chapters.text:
On the 17th day, Seal and Itzler went to the gym around 5:00 a.m. for a workout. The workouts greatly affected Itzler; he was becoming comfortable with his technique. For the first time, SEAL complimented him. Itzler had to go to work around 7:00 a.m. wearing the weight vest, which felt weird. The next day, they took the morning off, and at 7:00 p.m., they went to the gym and did 364 push-ups, 30 pull-ups, and 100 flutter kicks. On the 19th day, they went to Central Park at 7:00 a.m., intending to run six miles at a sub-eight-minute pace, with no mile over eight minutes.

Later that day, Itzler had an 11-hour workday followed by his company's holiday party. He was amazed seeing how much SEAL ate and how his workers were fascinated about having SEAL around. In addition, he was excited about drinking alcohol after a long abstinence. Despite the stressful day, Jesse Itzler knew he still had to work out and did 200 push-ups.

If you stick to your exercise routine and put in the work, you will start to see excellent results.

SEAL decided to focus on the basics on day 20 and had Itzler do ten push-ups followed by as many sit-ups as possible until the second hand on the clock hit 12 again, then push-ups and sit-ups again. They repeated that for 30 minutes nonstop; by the end of the day, Itzler had run for eight miles, done 400 push-ups, and 550 sit-ups. The next day Itzler only ran for 10 miles at an eight-minute-mile pace. One major thing Jesse Itzler learned from SEAL was about being present and able to focus on a task until you finish it. Mindfulness is something humans often lack in the modern day. We have too many distractions — especially digital ones. Sport is something that provides an excellent platform for developing better focus.

If you are hungry, run faster. You'll be home quicker. ~ SEAL

chapters.title: Constant training will become a habit
chapters.text:
On days 22 and 23, the Itzler family and SEAL went to Connecticut for the holidays. Itzler didn't work out on the 22nd day. However, at about 2:00 a.m. the following day, SEAL went to Itzler's room to talk to him about the 4/4/48 plan, which means running four miles every four hours for forty-eight hours. He ran for 4.5 miles in exactly 40 minutes, and four hours later, he did the same run in 38 minutes. But when it was time for run number three, Itzler had pain in his groin and couldn't run any further, so they switched to push-ups. He had run 16.1 miles and done 700 push-ups that day.

On the 24th day, a weather warning announced that no one should go out unless in an emergency; there were 16 inches of snow with 30-mph winds and –7 degrees with wind chill. SEAL seemed excited and insisted they run 3.5 miles in the mountains. Itzler came back home cold and frozen but did 154 push-ups afterward.

Making excuses is easy but futile; staying determined is tough and rewarding.

SEAL took Itzler on a five-mile run up Wanzer Hill in Connecticut on day 25. Itzler returned with pains in his foot, and SEAL asked him to go into the frozen lake. He was reluctant to dive into the lake but entered when SEAL yelled at him — that day ended with Itzler running for 9.5 miles, doing 775 push-ups and 125 sit-ups. Afterward, SEAL had him spend 21 minutes in the steam room at 25 degrees Fahrenheit, but Itzler was in such bad shape that they had to abort the mission. They went for the 4.5-mile mountain loop near the Itzler house the next day.

After the run, Itzler started talking more to SEAL and realized that SEAL had been ensuring his family's safety despite the training. SEAL later gave him time to tidy things up for the year, and afterward, they went for a five-mile run at top speed.

chapters.title: Consistency is key if you want to see results
chapters.text:
Jesse Itzler started day 27 with 1,000 push-ups; he was so excited, not because he did the push-ups but because he stuck with the journey. Then around 7:00 p.m., he headed out with SEAL for a 3.5-mile run.

Jesse Itzler's trip revealed that the body will respond if you push it.

They went for an 8.5-mile run up Leach Hollow Road the next day, then entered the frozen lake for a swim. At 3:00 p.m., Itzler did 300 push-ups. At about 8:00 p.m., while he and Sara were packing their luggage to go to Atlanta for a day trip, Sara started a conversation about SEAL. She asked when he was leaving, and Itzler said SEAL would leave in two days. Subsequently, Sara mentioned that she would miss SEAL and urged her husband to allow him to stay longer. On day 29, before the trip to Atlanta, Itzler and SEAL headed out on a 10.6-mile run in the rain from the house to a diner on Route 22. Later Jesse Itzler did 20 minutes on the treadmill, 100 push-ups, and 30 pull-ups before the trip.

On day 30, there was no morning routine because the Itzler family and SEAL had just arrived in New York from Atlanta. However, before lunchtime, they headed to Central Park for the 6.1-mile loop for their last run. As the day ended, Sara and Itzler were sad that their time with SEAL would end soon.

At the end of the training, Jesse Itzler realized he had become more robust and faster, and his mentality had changed. His body was in good shape, and he could do 1,000 push-ups seamlessly. On the 31st day, Jesse woke up at 8:00 a.m. to find that SEAL was gone. He went to the kitchen and saw a note from SEAL: “Hey man, thanks.”

Did you know? According to data from the Center for Disease Control's (CDC) National Center for Health Statistics, only 23% of American adults met leisure-time physical activity (LTPA) guidelines in 2018.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Sometimes, the difference between where we are and where we want to be is how much we have learned to control our minds. Your mind will lie to you about yourself and make you believe you're incapable of doing certain things, so take charge of it and feed it with what you want to see.

Jesse Itzler hired SEAL because he wanted to get in good shape, get out of his routine and leave his comfort zone. However, he got much more than he paid for. Aside from being fit, he realized that living with SEAL taught him many things he didn't bargain for. He learned how to view time differently, and instead of complaining and feeling frustrated, he now takes the posture of gratitude. His will to not give up easily also increased both in training and at work. After SEAL left, Itzler took time to admire SEAL's simplicity; how he did what he loved daily in a stress-free manner was a great lesson. As a result, he decided to concentrate on the essential things in life. The biggest thing he learned from SEAL was to appreciate difficult things.

As SEAL believed, everything is possible if we know how to undergo the necessary training to achieve.

Try this

Is there something you wish to do but are scared to, or feel it's out of the box? Well, it's time to leave your comfort zone and look for someone to train you to become what you desire. While in training, remain focused and committed and never give up. After a month, you will see significant change.

Read it till the end?
